docs.title: UNBL Documentation
docs.text:
Welcome to the UN Biodiversity Lab (UNBL) documentation. This guide will help you navigate and use the platform effectively.

Attention

We try to keep this documentation continuously updated. For the latest features and updates, visit the UNBL platform.

docs.title: About the Platform
docs.text:
The UN Biodiversity Lab (UNBL) is a collaborative initiative that brings together the best available spatial data on nature, climate change, and sustainable development. Our platform democratizes access to critical environmental data and analytics tools.

Key Features:

  • 400+ Global Datasets - Curated collection of authoritative spatial data
  • Dynamic Indicators - Real-time metrics for any country worldwide
  • No GIS Experience Required - User-friendly interface for all skill levels
  • Multilingual Support - Available in English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, and Russian
  • Secure Workspaces - Private collaborative environments for institutions
  • Open Source - Transparent, community-driven development

Partnership:

UNBL is a partnership between UNDP, UNEP, UNEP-WCMC, and the CBD Secretariat, working with technical partners and data providers to support evidence-based decision-making for nature and sustainable development.

docs.title: Support & Contact
docs.text:
For technical support, data questions, or workspace requests, contact support@unbiodiversitylab.org

Additional Resources:

  • UNBL Platform: unbiodiversitylab.org
  • Email Support: support@unbiodiversitylab.org
  • Data Request Form: Suggest new datasets
  • Workspace Request: Contact us

docs.title: Complete Documentation
docs.text:
The navigation structure is defined in mkdocs.yml. The documentation is organized into the following sections:

  • Getting Started: Registration, account management, navigation, and language settings
  • Map Navigation: Map controls, basemaps, and finding locations
  • Working with Data: Metrics, layers, time series, and customization
  • Sharing & Export: Data sharing, clipping, downloads, and map creation
  • Advanced Features: Digital public goods, workspaces, and data suggestions
  • Reference: Data catalog, troubleshooting, tutorials, and contributing guidelines

docs.title: How do I find additional data layers for my country?
docs.text:
Figure 1: How to find layers

Navigate to your area of interest, if preferred. You can also stay on the global view.

  1. Click on the LAYERS icon.
  2. To search for a layer, you can either:
  3. Type the name of the layer you want to view into the search box, and select the desired result in the layer list.
  4. Click to expand the layer tags, and select the data tag of interest. You then can select the desired layer from the layer list. OR
  5. Click to expand the filters box, and select your filter of interest. You then can select the desired place from the search result list.
  6. Click the toggle to the left of the layer name to load this layer to the map.
  7. Click the toggle again or click the X icon on the layer info to remove this layer.

Note: You can load multiple layers at the same time. You can also adjust the opacity of each layer by clicking on the layer name to open the layer info, and adjusting the opacity slider.

docs.title: How do I find the Digital Public Good (DPG) open data layers?
docs.text:
To find the Digital Public Good (DPG) open data layers in UNBL, follow these steps:

Figure 1: Find DPG layers

  1. Click on the LAYERS icon.
  2. Click to expand the filters box.
  3. Click to expand the Layer tags, you then can select the layer tag and layer category of interest.
  4. Click again the filter icon to visualize the complete list of layers that belong to your selected category, you then can select layer of interest from the layer result list.
  5. Click the toggle to the left of the layer name to load this layer to the map.
  6. Click the toggle again or click the X icon on the layer info to remove this layer.

docs.title: How do I find more information about each layer?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Find layer info

  1. Select the layer and load it to the map.
  2. On the left corner of the map, there will be a legend showing the name and symbology of the data layers on the map. Click on the icon to view the layer information. The information provides a description of the layer, source organization, citations, and links to download the data.

docs.title: How do I customize the layer views?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Customize map view

When selecting multiple layers, you can customize the map by adjusting their overlay order and opacity.

  1. To change the overlay order, click on the icon on the left of the layer name in the legend and move the icon up or down, as preferred. The top layer on the legend will be the top layer on the map.
  2. To change the opacity, click on the icon. Reducing the opacity increases the transparency of the layer. For example, to visualize both tree cover loss and protected areas, you can position the tree cover loss layer above the protected areas layer, and adjust the opacity of protected areas to 60%. This creates a map that shows tree cover loss within the protected areas, as well as the overall loss across the country.
  3. To temporarily hide a layer on the map, click on the icon. To make it visible again, click on the icon.

docs.title: What options do I have to visualize time series layers?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Time-Series Data

To visualize time series layers:

  1. Review our data list to confirm which layers are available as time series.
  2. Select the layer of interest.

  3. Customize based on the options available:

  4. Animation only: Click on the play icon to the left to see the animation of changes over this time period.

  5. Specific year only: Select the time (year, month, or date) you want to show on the map by clicking on the timeline bar.
  6. Customized animation: Select the time range (year, month, or date) you want to show on the map by clicking on the timeline bar. Click on the play icon to the left to see the animation of changes over this time period.

docs.title: How do I share a data layer?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Share Data

Sharing data layers is easy. To do so:

  1. Activate the layer(s) of interest on your map viewer.
  2. Copy the URL displayed in your browser.
  3. Share! Any data layers on our public platform will be accessible by anyone, regardless of whether they are a registered user.

docs.title: How do I clip and export layers?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Clip and Export

To clip a layer to your area of interest and download:

  1. Click on the PLACES icon and select your places of interest.
  2. Click on the … icon on the right of the country's name, and click on Clip and Export Layers.
  3. Type the name or select the data you want to download. If the data contains layers of multiple years, select the year you want to download.
  4. Click download.
  5. The selected data source will be clipped to the bounding box around the country.
  6. There is a small buffer added to the bounding box, which will slightly enlarge the area of the clipped raster. This helps to ensure that any incongruities between the national boundary used in UNBL and the official national boundary file you may wish to use do not result in loss of data. This assumes that differences are potentially small. If this is not the case, please contact us at support@unbiodiversitylab.org for assistance.
  7. Note: this is the raw data and will not include styling information.
  8. Access the downloaded .zip compressed file in your downloads folder once the download is complete.
  9. The downloaded data can be opened in any GIS software for further analysis.

docs.title: How do I download unclipped global data layers?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Download Global Data

  1. Select the layer of interest.
  2. Click on the layer info icon.
  3. Click on the link under LEARN MORE to download the data from its original source.

If you encounter any issues in accessing the data, please contact support@unbiodiversitylab.org for further support.

docs.title: How do I make a map for inclusion in reports and communication products?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Maps for Reports

  1. Customize the view of the layers you selected, as desired.
  2. Use the screenshot function on your computer to capture the map.
  3. For inclusion in formal reports or publications, please ensure you meet mapping standards:
  4. Maps should be clear and concise.
  5. Important text on maps should be clear and legible (e.g., cities, place names, legend items, etc.).
  6. The map should include basic mapping elements, including: a legend of relevant data layers and scale bar.
  7. The map must provide correct attribution(s) and citation(s) for the data source(s) used in its creation, either on the map itself or in a caption describing the map, e.g.:

Examples

if within the map:

Sources: Sanderman, et al., (2018); UNEP-WCMC and IUCN, (2021).

if in a caption:

Figure 2 shows the estimated soil organic carbon present in mangroves (from Sanderman, et al., (2018)) within protected areas in Vietnam (UNEP-WCMC and IUCN, (2021)).

Also be sure to include full citations for datasets used in the references/bibliography section of the document, e.g.:

Sanderman, J. et al. (2018) 'A global map of mangrove forest soil carbon at 30 m spatial resolution', Environmental Research Letters, 13(5), p. 055002. doi: 10.1088/1748-9326/aabe1c.

UNEP-WCMC and IUCN (2021), Protected Planet: The World Database on Protected Areas (WDPA) [On-line], 02/2021, Cambridge, UK: UNEP-WCMC and IUCN Available at: www.protectedplanet.net.

Include information on the map (or in the citation describing the map) on:

  • who created the map
  • the date the map was produced on UN Biodiversity Lab
  • and a note indicating that the map was created using UN Biodiversity Lab.

Examples

Created by James Bond using UN Biodiversity Lab (www.unbiodiversitylab.org) on 14 February 2021.

Finally, ensure that you also include a citation for the map in the references section of the document. We recommend this format:

Author. "Map title" [format]. Scale. (Date of Production). Map Generated on the UN Biodiversity Lab [web]. Version 2. UNDP and UNEP, [year].

Example

Bond, James. "Mangrove Forest Soil Organic Carbon within Vietnam's Protected Areas" [map]. Scale not given. (14 Feb 2021). Map Generated on the UN Biodiversity Lab (www.unbiodiversitylab.org) [web]. Version 2. UNDP and UNEP, 2023.

docs.title: How do I suggest more data for inclusion in the UN Biodiversity Lab?
docs.text:
We welcome suggestions on data that would enrich the UN Biodiversity Lab! If your data addresses biodiversity, conservation, or sustainable development at local, national, or international levels, please fill out this form.

Our team will review suggestions as they are received. UNDP, UN Environment Programme, and UN Environment Programme World Conservation Monitoring Centre (UNEP-WCMC) have adopted criteria to assist in identifying and curating the best available data layers on these themes in order to enable more streamlined and standardised analyses and reporting. These selection criteria will place data into one of three tiers, and consider aspects like data relevance, open-licensing and availability, transparency, and geographic coverage. We welcome feedback on these criteria (available here) to help inform their development and the accompanying methodology.

To upload data for your institution's use in a UNBL workspace, please see the next question on requesting a UNBL workspace.

docs.title: How do I register or log-in?
docs.text:
Figure 1: How to Register

Before you begin exploring the data, register for the UN Biodiversity Lab.

  1. Click the data page of the UN Biodiversity Lab website, then select the launch button to access the data app.

  2. Once this has loaded, select the account icon in the top right-hand corner and choose sign up. Enter your email, name, country, and institution (optional), and set your password to sign up.

  3. You will receive an email within a few minutes. Follow the instructions in this email to then follow email to verify your account.

  4. Once your account is verified, you can log in using your email address and password each time you access the platform.

  5. You can log-out at any time by clicking on your user icon and selecting Sign Out.

docs.title: What are UNBL workspaces? How do I request a workspace?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Private Workspaces

We recognize that national data is often higher quality and better suited to countries' needs for decision-making, monitoring, and reporting. UNBL offers free workspaces to stakeholders who need a secure area to access and use spatial data. Our workspaces can serve as a common data repository, offer a collaborative work environment, and enable you to calculate dynamic indicators for a sub-national or transboundary area of interest. These password-protected spaces enable you to control access to your data, and ensure data security through storage on UN servers.

We encourage users to explore how UNBL workspaces can help you to build communities of practice to transform data to action.

To request a secure UNBL workspace please contact support@unbiodiversitylab.org or click on the UNBL workspaces tab on our support page and fill out the form.

docs.title: How do I manage my account?
docs.text:
Figure 1: How to Manage Account

Once registered on the UN Biodiversity Lab, you will be able to manage your account, including editing your user name, email, password, country, and institution. You will also be able to view and edit the UNBL workspaces you belong to.

To manage your account:

  1. Click on the account icon with your initials on the top right, then click on profile.

  2. Click on the edit icon to edit your username, email, country, and institution.

  3. To reset your password, click on SEND RESET EMAIL, then follow the instructions in the email.

  4. To leave any of the UNBL workspaces you belong to, click on edit, then on leave workspace. Save your changes.

  5. If this account is no longer in use, you can click on DELETE YOUR ACCOUNT and the bottom of this page. After deleting the account, you will need to sign up again to gain registered user privileges on UN Biodiversity Lab.

  6. After saving your changes, click on RETURN TO MAP VIEW.

docs.title: How do I navigate between the UN Biodiversity Lab website and web map app?
docs.text:
Figure 1: How to Navigate

Navigating between the two pages is simple:

  1. To return to the UN Biodiversity Lab website from the data app, click the map view option and choose the home option on the top right of the panel.
  2. To navigate to the data app from the UN Biodiversity Lab website, click on Data.

docs.title: How do I change the language?
docs.text:
Figure 1: How to Change Language

The UN Biodiversity Lab is currently available in English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Russian. The default language is English.

To change the language, click on the account icon on the right corner of the map and click again to select the language you prefer from the dropdown menu.

You can change your language on either the UN Biodiversity Lab website or the data app.

docs.title: How do I adjust my map view?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Adjusting the Map View

There are several features that can assist you to navigate the map screen. These include:

  1. Move map: Use your mouse to drag the portion of the map you would like to view into the middle of the screen.
  2. Zoom in/out: Click on the ± icons at the bottom right of the map.
  3. Center place: Click on the center place button above ±. This will re-center the map over the selected place.
  4. Hide left menu bar: Click on the arrow at the top of the left menu to collapse the layer panel for a larger map view. Click again to expand the panel.

docs.title: How do I add/remove place labels, roads, and satellite view from the base map?
docs.text:
Figure 1: How to Manage Labels and Basemaps

There are several options for you to customize the base map. These include:

  1. Labels: The labels show the name of the places, including countries, states, cities, and representative landmarks. Click the toggle on to activate labels, and click off to hide.
  2. Roads: Click on the toggle to show roads; toggle off to hide roads.
  3. Map background: We offer grayscale and satellite options for the map background. To toggle between these options, click the small window at the left bottom right of the screen.

docs.title: How do I find my country?
docs.text:
Figure 1: How to Find a Country

The UN Biodiversity Lab can help you to navigate to a specific area of interest and access datasets and dynamic metrics for this area. On our public platform, areas of interest include countries, jurisdictions, and select transboundary areas. To search for an area of interest, you can either:

  1. Click on the PLACES icon, type the name of the country or jurisdiction you want to view into the search box, and select the desired result in the search result list. OR
  2. Click on the PLACES icon, click to expand the filters box, and select your filter of interest. You then can select the desired place from the search result list.

docs.title: What dynamic metrics are available for my country?
docs.text:
Figure 1: Viewing Dynamic Metrics

UN Biodiversity Lab offers at-a-glance metrics based on the best available UN-certified global spatial datasets. These metrics can be used to report on the state of nature and human development in your country. Available metrics include:

  • Tree cover loss (2001-2018)
  • Biodiversity intactness index (2015)
  • Enhanced vegetation index (2000-2019)
  • Global Land cover (2015)
  • Monthly fire activity(2018)
  • Protected areas (2019)
  • Terrestrial carbon density (2010)
  • Terrestrial human footprint (1993 & 2009)

To view the metrics on UN Biodiversity Lab:

  1. Select an area of interest.
  2. Review the metrics in the left panel.
  3. Click on the SHOW ON MAP icon if you want to view this layer on the map. Click on the REMOVE FROM MAP icon or the remove layer icon on the legend to clear the screen

docs.title: What dynamic metrics are available for my country?  (cont.)
docs.text:
Figure 1: How to Find a Country

  • Click on the icon to view layer info, the info pages provide a brief description of the data, related paper to read and source links.
  • To download summary data for the metric in .csv or .json format, click on the arrow icon . You can also download the data from source links on the layer's info pages.

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text: Credits and Thanks

docs.title: Additional Resources
docs.text: Links Books

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: How to Contribute Guidelines for Pull Requests Code of Conduct

docs.title: Basemap
docs.text: The background map layer that provides geographic context, showing features like country boundaries, terrain, or satellite imagery. Users can toggle between grayscale and satellite basemap options.

docs.title: Biodiversity Intactness Index
docs.text: A measure of how intact local biodiversity is relative to a pre-human baseline, expressed as a percentage. Values closer to 100% indicate ecosystems that are more similar to their natural state.

docs.title: CBD Secretariat
docs.text: The Secretariat of the Convention on Biological Diversity, the international treaty governing global biodiversity conservation efforts.

docs.title: Clip and Export
docs.text: A feature that allows users to download spatial data cropped to a specific geographic boundary, such as a country or administrative region.

docs.title: Coordinate System
docs.text: A reference framework used to define the positions of points on Earth's surface. Common systems include latitude/longitude (geographic coordinates) and various projected coordinate systems.

docs.title: Country Boundaries
docs.text: Administrative borders that define the territorial limits of nations, used in UNBL to provide country-specific data and metrics.

docs.title: Data App
docs.text: The interactive mapping application component of UNBL where users can visualize and analyze spatial data layers.

docs.title: Data Layers
docs.text: Individual datasets that can be visualized on the map, each representing different environmental, social, or economic variables. Examples include forest cover, protected areas, and population density.

docs.title: Digital Public Good (DPG)
docs.text: Open-source data, software, or content that advances sustainable development and is freely available for use. In UNBL, DPG layers are marked with special recognition for their open accessibility.

docs.title: Dynamic Metrics
docs.text: Real-time or regularly updated indicators calculated from spatial data that provide at-a-glance statistics for specific geographic areas, such as tree cover loss percentages or biodiversity indices.

docs.title: Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI)
docs.text: A satellite-based measurement of vegetation health and density that improves upon the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) by reducing atmospheric and soil background influences.

docs.title: Fire Activity
docs.text: Satellite-detected fire events and burn scars, typically measured monthly to track wildfire patterns and forest burning activities.

docs.title: GIS
docs.text: Geographic Information System - technology used to capture, store, analyze, and display geographically referenced data.

docs.title: Global Land Cover
docs.text: Classification of Earth's surface into different categories such as forests, croplands, urban areas, and water bodies, typically derived from satellite imagery.

docs.title: Human Industrial Footprint
docs.text: A measure of human (industrial-level) influence on the environment, combining factors like population density, infrastructure, agriculture, and land use change to assess cumulative human impact.

docs.title: Jurisdictions
docs.text: Administrative or legal regions with defined boundaries, including countries, states, provinces, and other governmental territories.

docs.title: Layer Overlay
docs.text: The arrangement and stacking order of multiple data layers on the map, where users can control which layers appear on top and how they interact visually.

docs.title: Legend
docs.text: The map key that explains the symbols, colors, and patterns used to represent different values or categories in the displayed data layers.

docs.title: Map View
docs.text: The main interactive interface displaying geographic data, including controls for zooming, panning, and layer management.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Descriptive information about datasets, including source, methodology, temporal coverage, spatial resolution, and data quality assessments.

docs.title: Opacity
docs.text: The transparency level of data layers, which can be adjusted to allow underlying layers to show through while maintaining visibility of the top layer.

docs.title: Place Labels
docs.text: Text annotations on the map showing names of geographic features like countries, cities, states, and landmarks.

docs.title: Projection
docs.text: A mathematical method for representing the curved surface of Earth on a flat map, with different projections optimizing for different properties like area, distance, or direction. Related to Coordinate Reference Systems

docs.title: Protected Areas
docs.text: Legally designated regions managed for biodiversity conservation, including national parks, nature reserves, and other conservation areas as defined by the World Database on Protected Areas (WDPA).

docs.title: Raster Data
docs.text: Spatial data represented as a grid of cells or pixels, where each cell contains a value representing information about that location, commonly used for satellite imagery and environmental variables.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The level of detail in spatial data, typically measured as the size of the smallest unit (pixel) that can be distinguished, expressed in meters (e.g., 30m resolution means each pixel represents a 30×30 meter area).

docs.title: Satellite View
docs.text: A basemap option showing satellite or aerial imagery of Earth's surface, providing visual context for data analysis.

docs.title: Spatial Data
docs.text: Information that has a geographic component, meaning it can be mapped and analyzed in relation to specific locations on Earth.

docs.title: Terrestrial Carbon Density
docs.text: The amount of carbon stored in above and below-ground biomass and soil per unit area, typically measured in tons per hectare.

docs.title: Time Series
docs.text: Data collected at multiple time points, allowing users to visualize and analyze changes over time through animations or by selecting specific years or date ranges.

docs.title: Transboundary Areas
docs.text: Regions that span across national borders, often referring to shared ecosystems, watersheds, or conservation areas managed cooperatively by multiple countries.

docs.title: Tree Cover Loss
docs.text: The removal or mortality of tree canopy cover, measured annually using satellite data to track deforestation and forest degradation patterns.

docs.title: UN Biodiversity Lab (UNBL){#unbl}
docs.text: A collaborative platform that democratizes access to spatial data and analytic tools to support evidence-based decision-making for conservation and sustainable development.

docs.title: UNDP
docs.text: United Nations Development Programme - the UN agency focused on sustainable development and poverty reduction.

docs.title: UNEP
docs.text: United Nations Environment Programme - the UN agency responsible for coordinating environmental activities and assisting countries in implementing environmentally sound policies.

docs.title: UNEP-WCMC
docs.text: UN Environment Programme World Conservation Monitoring Centre - the specialist biodiversity assessment centre of UNEP that provides information services on conservation and sustainable use of species and ecosystems.

docs.title: Vector Data
docs.text: Spatial data represented using geometric shapes (points, lines, and polygons) to represent geographic features, commonly used for boundaries, roads, and discrete locations.

docs.title: Workspace
docs.text: A secure, password-protected collaborative environment within UNBL where institutions can upload, share, and analyze their own datasets alongside global data layers.

docs.title: Zoom
docs.text: The ability to change the scale of the map view to show more detail (zoom in) or a broader geographic area (zoom out), controlled through map interface buttons or mouse interactions.

docs.title: Who we are
docs.text: The UN Biodiversity Lab (UNBL) supports country-led efforts to use spatial data and analytic tools to generate insight and impact for conservation and sustainable development. Our mission is three-fold: (1) to democratize access to spatial data and analytic tools as a digital public good; (2) to support decision-makers to leverage spatial data for insight, priority-setting, and implementation at the national level; and (3) to empower stakeholders to use spatial data for nationally-led monitoring and reporting.

docs.title: The Platform
docs.text:
With over 400 of the world's best data layers on nature, climate change, and sustainable development, UNBL enables policymakers and other stakeholders to use spatial data to take action for people and planet.

The platform provides the ability for users to:

  • Visualize core global public good datasets at the heart of decision-making on nature and sustainable development
  • Access curated collections that integrate spatial data for insight and action
  • View and download dynamic indicators of change for any country in the world
  • Create workspaces to securely upload national data for analysis alongside global data
  • Develop communities of practice that nurture data transparency and cross-sectoral collaboration
  • Draw on the expertise of UNBL partners to develop national strategies and plans

UNBL is a free, open-source environment that does not require any previous GIS experience. Powered by UNEP-WCMC, UNBL works to harness emerging technologies and innovation to support state-of-the art infrastructure and user-centric design.

docs.title: The partnership
docs.text: UNDP, UNEP and its specialist biodiversity centre UNEP-WCMC, and the CBD Secretariat – together with our technical partners and data providers – bring together data and users to create innovative solutions to address our planetary emergency. Through our partnership, we engage nearly 200 countries, allowing for dynamic updates to the platform based on user needs. Relationships with technical partners and data providers ensures the provision of cutting-edge tools and data to take action for nature and sustainable development. Together, we align our work to the UN Common Approach for Biodiversity and the UN Secretary General's Data Strategy, leveraging open data to create a solution-driven, user-friendly platform.

docs.title: Convening partners
docs.text: The UNBL convening partners work in dynamic partnership to ensure all stakeholders can generate insights from spatial data to take action for nature and sustainable development.

docs.title: Data
docs.text:
UNBL is not the producer of the data it displays does not directly license the datasets used on this platform. We provide access to data subject to the conditions of use of the source datasets themselves. Licensing information for each layer can be found on each layer's information page.

Digital public good (DPG) open data - All datasets available under the "DPG" recognition are available under one of the following licenses:

  • CC-BY-4.0
  • CC-BY-SA-4.0
  • CC0-1.0
  • ODbL-1.0
  • ODC-By-1.0
  • PDDL-1.0
  • CC-BY-3.0-IGO
  • CC-BY-SA-3.0-IGO

docs.title: Content
docs.text: Unless otherwise specified, all content available in the "Resources" section and on this website is under CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0 (Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial Share Alike 4.0 International). This means you are free to share and adapt the content, under the terms of properly attributing credits to the source and/or author (UNBL or the copyright holder of the asset).

docs.title: Copyright
docs.text: UNBL does not assert intellectual property rights in the data made available by Third-Party Data providers for inclusion on the platform. However, all intellectual property rights for the platform are the property of UNBL and/or its licensors. All intellectual property rights are reserved.
